fragments.content: Prostate cancer is the most common cancer in men in developed countries. PSA (prostate-specific antigen) screening is controversial due to overdiagnosis of indolent cancers. Risk stratification guides management: very low/low risk (PSA <10, Gleason 6, T1-T2a, <50% cores positive); favorable intermediate (one intermediate factor); unfavorable intermediate (multiple intermediate factors or Gleason 4 pattern predominant); high (PSA >20, Gleason 8-9, T3); very high (T4, Gleason 10, primary Gleason 5). Gleason scoring (Grade Groups 1-5): Grade Group 1 (Gleason 3+3=6), 2 (3+4=7), 3 (4+3=7), 4 (8), 5 (9-10); Grade Group predicts prognosis. Genomic classifiers (Decipher, Oncotype DX Prostate, Prolaris) refine risk assessment. Molecular features: TMPRSS2-ERG fusion (~50%), PTEN loss, TP53 mutations in advanced disease; HRR gene mutations (BRCA2, ATM, others) in 10-20% metastatic; MSI-H rare (<3%).

Management depends on risk and life expectancy. Very low/low risk, life expectancy >10 years: active surveillance (AS) preferred - PSA every 3-6 months, DRE annually, repeat biopsy every 1-3 years; curative treatment if reclassification. Intermediate risk: radiation therapy (EBRT or brachytherapy) + short-term ADT (4-6 months) or radical prostatectomy; some favorable intermediate may choose AS. High/very high risk: radiation + long-term ADT (18-36 months) or radical prostatectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection; consider adding abiraterone for very high risk (STAMPEDE trial). Metastatic hormone-sensitive: ADT + intensification - docetaxel (CHAARTED), abiraterone (LATITUDE), apalutamide (TITAN), or enzalutamide (ENZAMET); triplet therapy (ADT + docetaxel + darolutamide/abiraterone) for high-volume disease (ARASENS, PEACE-1). Castration-resistant: non-metastatic - apalutamide, enzalutamide, or darolutamide (add to ADT); metastatic - docetaxel, cabazitaxel, abiraterone, enzalutamide, radium-223 (bone-predominant), lutetium-177-PSMA (VISION trial), PARP inhibitors for HRR-mutated (PROfound, TRITON3).

Diagnosis requires tissue confirmation. PSA elevation triggers evaluation: consider age-specific ranges, PSA velocity, free/total PSA ratio, PHI (Prostate Health Index), 4Kscore. Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) increasingly used before biopsy; PI-RADS score 1-5 predicts clinically significant cancer; MRI-targeted biopsy superior to systematic alone (PRECISION trial). Transrectal or transperineal biopsy: systematic 12-core standard; MRI-targeted cores added for suspicious lesions. Pathology: Gleason score from biopsy/surgical specimen; percent core involvement; perineural invasion. Staging: bone scan and CT/MRI for high-risk disease or symptoms; PSMA PET/CT increasingly used (more sensitive than conventional imaging, detects recurrence at lower PSA). Post-prostatectomy monitoring: PSA should be undetectable (<0.1); rising PSA indicates recurrence. Biochemical recurrence definition: post-radiation PSA nadir + 2 ng/mL (Phoenix criteria); post-prostatectomy PSA ≥0.2 ng/mL confirmed.
